text: Which Article of the Constitution establishes the Parliament of India?
explanation: Article 79 outlines the constitution of Parliament.

text: The Parliament of India consists of which of the following?
options: President, Lok Sabha, and Rajya Sabha
Lok Sabha, Rajya Sabha, and Supreme Court
explanation: The Parliament comprises the President, the Lok Sabha, and the Rajya Sabha.

text: Which committee recommended that the President be considered a part of the Parliament?
explanation: The N. Gopalaswami Ayyangar Committee recommended making the President an integral part of Parliament.

text: Why is the President considered a part of the Parliament?
options: No bill becomes a law without his assent
He is the head of the majority party
explanation: Because no bill passed by the chambers can become law without the President's approval.

text: Which Article specifically details the structure of the Lok Sabha?
explanation: Article 81 details the composition of the Lok Sabha.

text: Which of these is NOT an alternative name for the Lok Sabha?
explanation: The Upper House refers to the Rajya Sabha, not the Lok Sabha.

text: Why is the Lok Sabha called the 'Temporary House'?
options: It functions for only a few days a year
It does not have real power
It is housed in a temporary building
explanation: It is called a Temporary House because it can be dissolved by the President before its term ends.

text: When was the Parliament of India first constituted after independence?
explanation: The first Parliament was constituted in April 1952 following the first general elections.

text: What was the initial English name of the Lok Sabha?
explanation: It was initially named the 'House of People'.

text: When was the name 'House of People' changed to 'Lok Sabha'?
explanation: The name was changed to Lok Sabha on 14 May 1954.

text: During the first general elections (1951-52), what was the maximum strength of the Lok Sabha?
explanation: The maximum strength of the Lok Sabha during the first general elections was set at 500.

text: How many seats were actually contested in the first Lok Sabha elections?
explanation: Elections were held for 489 seats.

text: During the first general elections, how many triple-member constituencies existed?
explanation: There was exactly one triple-member constituency (in North Bengal).

text: When was the multi-member constituency system abolished?
explanation: The multi-member system was abolished in 1961, before the 1962 elections.

text: The 7th Constitutional Amendment (1956) increased the Lok Sabha seats from 500 to?
explanation: The 7th Amendment raised the max seats from 500 to 525 after state reorganization.

text: The 31st Constitutional Amendment (1973) increased the seats to?
explanation: The 31st Amendment increased the seats from 525 to 545.

text: Which act set the maximum limit of Lok Sabha seats to 552?
options: Goa, Daman and Diu Reorganisation Act (1987)
explanation: The Goa, Daman and Diu Reorganisation Act of 1987 set the limit at 552.

text: According to the 84th Constitutional Amendment (2002), Lok Sabha seats are frozen until which year?
explanation: The seats are frozen until the year 2026.

text: Before the 104th Amendment, how many Anglo-Indians could the President nominate to the Lok Sabha?
explanation: Under Article 331, the President could nominate 2 Anglo-Indians.

text: Which constitutional amendment discontinued the nomination of Anglo-Indians to the Lok Sabha?
explanation: The 104th Constitutional Amendment (December 2019) ended the Anglo-Indian nomination.

text: What is the maximum current possible strength of the Lok Sabha after the 104th Amendment?
explanation: The maximum strength is now 550 (552 minus the 2 Anglo-Indian seats).

text: What is the current base census year used to decide population thresholds for Lok Sabha seat distributions?
explanation: The distribution of seats is frozen based on the 1971 census until 2026.

text: Who is known as the 'Popular House'?
explanation: The Lok Sabha relies on direct popular election, hence it is the Popular House.

text: In the 1951-52 elections, how many double-member constituencies were there?
explanation: There were 86 double-member constituencies.

text: How many total constituencies (electoral districts) were there in the first general elections?
explanation: There were 401 constituencies (314 single, 86 double, 1 triple) yielding 489 seats.
